symbols: lion resting at his feet — Jerome's legendary companion, tamed after he extracted a thorn from its paw, symbolizing the power of Christian charity over wild nature
crucifix on the desk — the centrality of Christ's Passion to Jerome's scholarly and devotional life
hourglass — the passage of time and mortality, a vanitas reminder even amid intellectual pursuits
cardinal's red robes and biretta — Jerome's traditional (though anachronistic) rank as a cardinal, representing his authority as a Doctor of the Church
open books and writing implements — Jerome's monumental labor of translating Scripture into the Latin Vulgate
small Madonna and Child painting on the wall — Marian devotion as companion to scriptural study
numerous animals (pheasants, deer, partridges, parrot, rabbits) — the peaceable kingdom, creation at harmony under the scholar-saint's sanctified presence
antlers mounted as chandelier — the stag as symbol of the soul thirsting for God (Psalm 42)
description: A richly furnished Renaissance study with green-beamed ceiling and a large mullioned window at right. A man in cardinal's red robes and biretta sits at a broad wooden desk, writing or reading. Before him on the desk stand a small crucifix, an hourglass, books, and various objects. A small painting of the Madonna and Child hangs on the wall behind him. A parrot perches on the desk edge at left. On the floor below, a large tawny lion reclines at rest. Scattered around the room are numerous birds including pheasants and partridges, as well as rabbits and a deer.
theological_description: This painting belongs to the rich tradition of depicting St. Jerome in his study, but with the distinctive conceit of portraying Cardinal Albrecht of Brandenburg in the saint's role -- a form of devotional identification common in Northern Renaissance patronage. Jerome (c. 347-420) was the supreme biblical scholar of the Latin West, whose translation of Scripture into the Vulgate became the Church's authoritative Bible for over a millennium. The study setting emphasizes the vita contemplativa: Jerome withdrew from Roman ecclesiastical politics to a monastic cell in Bethlehem, where he devoted decades to the painstaking work of rendering Hebrew and Greek into Latin. The crucifix and hourglass form a devotional pair -- Christ's sacrifice gives meaning to the passing hours of mortal scholarly labor. The lion, Jerome's most famous attribute, derives from a medieval legend in which the saint healed a lion's wounded paw, after which the beast became his faithful companion; theologically it signifies that even ferocious nature is subdued by sanctity and charity. The profusion of animals populating the study evokes a kind of Edenic harmony, suggesting that the scholar immersed in God's Word participates in the restoration of creation's original peace. The stag antlers overhead allude to Psalm 42:1, 'As the deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God' -- the driving thirst behind all authentic biblical scholarship.

scripture_refs.reason: The stag antlers mounted as chandelier evoke the deer thirsting for God, symbolizing Jerome's insatiable desire for divine wisdom through Scripture
The charge to be a worker who rightly handles the word of truth -- the defining mission of Jerome's life as translator and exegete
God's Word as a lamp and light -- Jerome's study illuminated by Scripture embodies this psalm of devotion to the Torah
The peaceable kingdom where the lion lies down with other creatures -- the animals in Jerome's study prefigure the messianic restoration of creation
The vanitas elements (hourglass, passing time) amid scholarly labor echo Ecclesiastes' reflection on the endless making of books

_meta.title: Albrecht of Brandenburg as St. Jerome in his study
